nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q1.alt: Question 1. Which information helps the reader understand the meaning of “cherish” in paragraph 6? Choices: A: Kincaid’s memories are of happy times. B: Kincaid’s memories are about outdoor activities. C: Kincaid’s memories are about learning new things. D: Kincaid’s memories are of people she will likely see again.

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q2.alt: Question 2. Based on paragraphs 12 through 19, which sentence best explains the difference in the characters’ reactions to the objects in the trunk? Choices: A: The objects seem ragged to one character, while to the other character, they seem new. B: The objects seem to be costly to one character, while to the other character, they seem to be inexpensive. C: The objects are familiar reminders to one character, while to the other character, they are exciting discoveries. D: The objects are family treasures to one character, while to the other character, they are personal belongings.

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q3.alt: Question 3. In paragraph 19, what does the narrator mean when she says, “her laughter floated through the house sweet as the taste of jellybeans”? Choices: A: The laughter reminds Kincaid of the candy Grandma Talley keeps at her house. B: The sound of Grandma Talley’s laughter gives Kincaid a light and pleasant feeling. C: The sound of Grandma Talley’s laughter can be enjoyed by others inside the house. D: The laughter reminds Kincaid of how kind and thoughtful Grandma Talley has always been.

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q4.alt: Question 4. Which detail best shows what Grandma Talley thinks about change? Choices: A: “You still frettin’ about moving out of state’cause of your mama’s job?” (paragraph 1) B: “I know, Kincaid, but things work out, most times better than we expect.” (paragraph 3) C: “I’ve taught you the way my grandma taught me, like her grandmother before her, passing on our stories to those coming after us.” (paragraph 21) D: “‘This was taken from my wedding dress when I married your Grandpa Wilford,’ she said touching a patch of satin the color of ecru.” (paragraph 24)

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q5.alt: Question 5. Read Grandma Talley’s words from paragraph 26 of the story. “Remember, Kincaid, we take our loved ones in our hearts wherever we go. I won’t be more than a heartbeat away.” When Grandma Talley says she “won’t be more than a heartbeat away,” she means she won’t seem far because Choices: A: Kincaid can always think about her B: she and Kincaid are blood relatives C: she will be thinking of Kincaid all the time D: Kincaid can always call her on the telephone

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q6.alt: Question 6. Which words from the story best show Grandma Talley’s sense of humor? Choices: A: “Scat, get away from here now, causing trouble,” (paragraph 9) B: “Grandma Talley smiled warmly, dragging out an old trunk.” (paragraph 12) C: “Yes, still wet behind the ears,” (paragraph 16) D: “She smiled, hugging me tightly.” (paragraph 26)

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q7.alt: Question 7. Which paragraph best expresses a theme of the story? Choices: A: paragraph 10 B: paragraph 13 C: paragraph 20 D: paragraph 23

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q8.alt: Question 8. The word “collide” comes from a Latin word meaning “strike together.” Based on this information, what is the meaning of “collide” in paragraph 2? Choices: A: bump into with force B: hit with an object C: injure by bruising D: swing against

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q9.alt: Question 9. Which paragraph does the photograph of the racers best help the reader understand? Choices: A: paragraph 1 B: paragraph 2 C: paragraph 3 D: paragraph 4

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q10.alt: Question 10. The information in paragraphs 7 and 8 best supports the idea that manufacturers develop Choices: A: equipment to introduce a new sport B: equipment when there is an existing need C: products when the old ones are not safe D: products when the old ones are not purchased

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q11.alt: Question 11. As they are used in paragraph 10, what do the words “trick out” mean? Choices: A: The rider adds fancy trim and wheels. B: The rider spends a large amount of money. C: The rider adds parts to improve performance. D: The rider makes changes that create a unique appearance.

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q12.alt: Question 12. Read these sentences from paragraphs 2 and 11. You have to be an outstanding rider for BMX racing. If you want to race, a good rule is to buy the best bike you can afford. What can the reader conclude from these sentences? Choices: A: Tricks and expensive gear make BMX racing appealing. B: BMX racers need practice and money to be successful. C: Skill and good equipment are important in BMX racing. D: BMX racers will win with the right preparation and tools.

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q13.alt: Question 13. Which of these is more important to BMX racing than to freestyle BMX? Choices: A: danger B: difficulty C: expense D: speed

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q14.alt: Question 14. Based on the information in the passage, how would freestyle BMX best prepare a rider for BMX racing? Choices: A: by helping the rider develop more skills B: by helping the rider stay in good condition C: by helping the rider escape serious injury D: by helping the rider avoid harmful crashes

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q15.alt: Question 15. In paragraph 7, what does the author’s use of the phrase “picked up the pace and flashed me a playful smile” indicate? Choices: A: Anna and Supphatra need to hurry to finish their shopping. B: Anna has difficulty keeping up with Supphatra in the unfamiliar place. C: Supphatra enjoys the time she spends shopping with Anna. D: Supphatra expects that Anna will be surprised by what happens next.

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q16.alt: Question 16. Which word best describes how Supphatra is feeling in paragraph 12? Choices: A: confident B: confused C: disappointed D: friendly

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q17.alt: Question 17. How does paragraph 14 fit into the structure of the story? Choices: A: It resolves the conflict that is presented in paragraph 13. B: It explains a problem that is solved in paragraph 15. C: It introduces the feelings of the narrator. D: It adds mystery to the events.

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q18.alt: Question 18. Read this sentence from paragraph 15 of the story. Timidly, I scooped a little onto my plate, followed by some curried meats and vegetables. What does the word “timidly” suggest? Choices: A: unhappiness B: uncertainty C: friendliness D: bravery

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q19.alt: Question 19. Which sentence best supports the theme of the story? Choices: A: “Since our family moved to Bangkok six months ago, I had learned many things.” (paragraph 4) B: “I’ve never been very brave when it comes to trying new foods.” (paragraph 6) C: “She closed her eyes and smiled contentedly.” (paragraph 8) D: “My heart thumped wildly in my chest.” (paragraph 15)

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q20.alt: Question 20. Why does the author most likely include both paragraphs 16 and 17? Choices: A: to contrast for the reader Anna’s earlier fears and her enjoyment of the food B: to explain to the reader why Supphatra’s family thinks Anna is funny C: to help the reader understand the different flavors in Thai dishes D: to show the reader how delicious Thai food is

nysed-ela-2014-g5-mc-q21.alt: Question 21. What does the narrator’s description in paragraph 18 most likely suggest about Anna? Choices: A: She is relieved that the dinner has ended. B: She accepts that her friend is different from her. C: She is determined to try unusual foods. D: She is curious about her friend’s actions.
